_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Stimulant Poisoning/Overdose. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Stimulant Poisoning/Overdose

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Stimulant Poisoning/Overdose
points: Protect organs at risk for injury such as heart, brain, liver, kidney
Determine if there is an antidote
Treat the symptoms, which may include severe tachycardia and hypertension, agitation, hallucinations, chest pain, seizure, and arrhythmia

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Apply the least amount of physical management devices that are necessary to protect the patient and the clinicians [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline ]
Assessment for potential weapons or additional drugs is very important since these items can pose a threat not just to the patient but also to the EMS crew

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Recognition and treatment of hyperthermia (including sedatives to decrease heat production from muscular activity) is essential as many deaths are attributable to hyperthermia

If law enforcement has placed the patient in handcuffs, this patient needs ongoing physical security for safe transport. Have law enforcement in back of ambulance for the handcuffed patient or make sure proper non-handcuff physical management devices are in place before law enforcement leaves and ambulance departs from scene

If patient has signs and symptoms of ACS, consider giving nitroglycerin sublingual (SL) q (quaque, every) 3'5 minutes if SBP greater than 100 mmHg and until pain resolves (if range not desired, use q 3 minutes) a. Vasospasm is often the problem in this case as opposed to a fixed coronary artery lesion

Maintaining IV access, cardiac monitor, and SPO2/EtCO2 monitors are key to being able to catch and intervene decompensations in a timely manner a. If agitated, consider restraining the patient to facilitate patient assessment and lessen likelihood of vascular access or monitor displacements

Cocaine has sodium channel blocking effects and can cause significant cardiac conduction abnormalities with a widened QRS. Treatment is with sodium bicarbonate similar to a tricyclic antidepressant. Check a 12-lead EKG to assess for these complications
History is as important as the physical examination.
If the patient is on psychiatric medication, but has failed to be compliant, this fact alone puts the patient at higher risk for the adverse outcome of delirium with agitated behavior

If the patient is found naked, this may elevate the suspicion for stimulant use or abuse. These substances increase the risk for sudden death secondary to delirium with agitated behavior. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome or serotonin syndrome can present with similar signs and symptoms

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Reason for psychologic and physical management procedures used and neurologic/circulatory exams with device use
' Documentation of QT interval when antiemetic medications, haloperidol, or droperidol is used and result conveyed to ED staff

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Apply the least amount of physical management devices that are necessary to protect the patient and the clinicians [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline ]
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
